replies.text: I understand his admiration for many elements in the film, but when he asserts "obviously a lot of thought was put into it," I'm perplexed.  There is a lot of talent on display, but the giant weakness of the movie, which ruined it for me, is the lack of thought put into the plot, which is riddled with giant holes.  Why isn't law enforcement more suspicious of Alex's family?  His parents have both been absent from their jobs for over a month.  Someone has covered the house's windows with newspapers, yet Julia Garner is able to spy two catatonic figures spookily sitting at the dinner table.  In fact, both Julia Garner and Josh Brolin are making decent headway in their investigations, but the FBI and the local police have no leads, no suspects.  I don't see how this can be seen as an exploration of alcoholism when Julia Garner's drinking is only shown at the beginning of the film and not really developed thereafter.  In fact, the structure (which reminded me of the Tarantino-light movie "Go" more than anything else) robs us of what the trailer promised:  the tension of a protagonist unfairly under suspicion of the townspeople.  That is established in the first section--we get the word "WITCH" painted on her car--but it is missing from the sections about Alden Ehrenreich's policeman, the hilarious drug addict, the principal, and Alex and his aunt.  That premise has great potential:  When the mob is unfairly accusing you of something heinous, you start to doubt yourself, especially if you are inebriated much of the time.  An innocent person can begin to feel guilty.  I felt robbed.

replies.text:
@FrankLee20036 might be reading too much into the movie where kids rip off the face of a witch, but I kept thinking to myself why the movie is called Weapons and how the assault rifle in Brolin’s nightmare sequence ties into it. Then I fell down a mental rabbit hole.

With that imagery and a bunch of kids vanishing, this movie is obviously about school shootings in some way. But I think the movie takes it a step further and is really about right wing indoctrination and radicalization. 

First, there are multiple references to parasites in the movie, which is probably just referring to Gladys inserting and taking control of the household. But, I think that could represent the inserting of right-wing rhetoric into the minds of kids. Gladys zombifies Alex’s parents, which made me think of the absentee parents you see in these types of stories, oblivious to what is happening with their kid. Gladys also says that whatever she is doing to the parents (something with their souls?) isn’t enough for her , she needs kids. The right wing has been especially reliant in recent years in changing/gutting education and getting into the minds of young people (Gen Alpha and the younger end of Gen Z) in order for their ideology to remain and spread (like a parasite).

I think the line where Josh Brolin says something like “it’s weaponizing our kids” summarizes what Creeger is trying to say. This radicalization turns kids into weapons, sometimes even literally with shootings. I think it is also intentional how Gladys kills a gay couple in the middle of the movie (otherwise, why make Benedict Wong’s character gay?). I think Creeger is intentional in also showing that Alex is bullied and ostracized by his class, as similar things happen with a lot of young right wingers, whose anger and loneliness are exploited by influencers and the like. (This also may be the reason why in the beginning, one of the first lines of dialogue is about feeling angry and explaining how universal it can be).

This read isn’t perfect. I don’t know how a lot of the other characters really fit into this puzzle.  Alex, even though he is responsible partially for the missing kids, isn’t vengeful really or looking to hurt them (unlike a lot of school shooters) and only does what he does to save his parents. And also I don’t know why the right wing influences would be represented by an old witch instead of something else.

Anyway, thought that would be worthwhile to say because I’ve seen people say this movie isn’t about anything, but I don’t think that’s the case.

My reading of the movie was it was about gun violence, especially mass shootings in schools, along with blamimg the police and older generations for what's happening.

The film starts with an emphasis on the kids, even being narrated by a kid (I couldn't figure out who this was supposed to be). The town wants to blame the teacher as she's the only one who could be a scapegoat. At the beginning, the town hall latches onto her the same way a minority group would get blamed for a mass shooting rather than the weapons (lol).

In the third act, after the introduction of Gladys, the film pivots into a critique of the elderly, their current parasitic nature to younger generations and their lack of giving a shit. When Alex's house all falls to shit at the end, her first thought is ditching the home and skipping town.

I could add on about the police stuff but the dash cam footage scene with the chief should be evidence enough in the text.

Yeah, he could say it's about a lot of things because of how well he incorporates the idea of communal loss, social trauma, and how people live through it. 

However, when you put a GIANT AR-14 floating above a house in a dream sequence with the numbers 2:17 on the side of the gun (in a movie that doesn't have gun violence), it's impossible not to see the connections to the sense of loss and pain a community feels when a large segment of the population is suddenly "gone." The themes are there even if they are not the primary or even the secondary focus of the film. There's this undercurrent of mass loss that you simply can't escape.

I went into it knowing next to nothing about it. I didn't draw any analogies to any particular social woes, but I can see how it could be interpreted as a school shooting allegory. People are entitled to their own interpretations as long as they don't insist theirs is the absolute correct one.

That being said, holy Keeee-RISTE, this was the best movie I've seen in many years. It succeeds on all levels, and it's enjoyable from beginning to end. My only complaint is that it's raised the bar for other films. I almost don't want to see any other movies for a while, knowing they won't even approach this.

author: @youknowwhoiam2771
text: There were many scenes which had tremendous horror elements but one scene in particular sticks with me - Miss Gandy is waiting outside Alex’s house. Cut to night and she’s black out drunk passed out. The camera pans to the house and the door opens. From the darkness a woman steps out, a blade in hand and her movements inhuman. She approaches Gandy’s car and then just stops. It could have easily been played for a laugh. The woman steps out of frame and the viewer can’t see her. All they can hear is the sound of the door being opened. There is something in the back. And then a hand with scissors comes into view and cuts off a lock of Gandy’s hair. And that was it. No cut to a scary face, no excessive gore. Just the knowledge of something being there when you are completely defenceless and an unseen horror which could have done anything to you…

​@TheGekko135 that is true. Brolin is a producer. I suspect as well intended as was the project, they watered down the horror, so that he doesn't kill (the head of school, the junkie, or even the teacher - that would have been sick, and well into the first part, dramatically and psychologically mature) and that he's find his son back.

But he had to 'win'... As the nephew's parents had to survive. So i suspect this is more a producer-star project, than a film-maker project. And i don't complain. I like 'happy ending' (or family) horror,.like with the first Warren Files. But that one began more like a thriller, and would have been better to remain true to ( i suppose) its writers' idea. They must have tried to stay even with the witch eaten by her uncontrolled preys, but she better have died burnt or heart-pierced, and some preys eaten... 
Still, good psychology (except for going in that house without calling the cops, telling them one of their own was missing! which the precinct would know at this point).

replies.text:
⁠​⁠​⁠​⁠​⁠​⁠@hapahapahappy I think the problem I had with the movie, which may also be the same for others, was that I was heavily invested in the mistery from the beginning, but then the tangential stories from each character's pov eventually felt like too much... 
The beginning got me hooked with everything happening to the teacher, like being stalked/haunted in her house, her car being vandalized etc., seemed like things were escalating... But then there's the whole affair thing with the police officer, which, alright I get it that it's building the characters and the world around them and also making a point of where the stories connect later on, but it seemed like some of it was kinda pointless, and it just felt like a drag... Then after that every other character chapter slowly made me feel the same way.
I also thought it was way more funny than scary - the jumpscares with the villain's face and her weird hair and makeup; Wong's face when he is running after the teacher; the kids running after the villain in the end completely wrecking the houses and breaking every possible window, and then doing a whole fatality on that woman despite being on a soup diet for a long time... come on man that stuff was way too funny
Oh well, maybe the movie wasn't for me

We loved it. I’m seeing some people say it’s their fav of the year while others crapping all over it

Horror is hard to get right so you can’t please everyone.

But I loved the originality of it. I also respect that the marketing gave away NOTHING.

Brolin nails every scene he’s in, Garner played a trouble school teacher and boy I did NOT see the explanation of why the kids went missing coming

Great film and happy the Drinker enjoyed it as much as I did 

Cheers

author: @Longshanks1690
text:
Weapons was an effective and compelling story which is suspenseful and terrifying when it wants to be, with a mystery that feels grounded and satisfying enough when you get to the end. Alex's actor especially was brilliant and able to hold the whole third act effectively on his own. 

But the writing really isn't as strong as it could be.
Why was the witch tormenting the teacher and dad with nightmares when she was trying to keep a low profile?
Why was the police response so lacklustre, when an event of this scale justifies thermal imagery to find the kids?
Why did no one else's Ring cameras track them running to Alex's house?
Who knocked on the teacher's door and wrote Witch? (Presumably it was the dad.)
Why does the witch do all this? (Presumably to get younger, but that was only subtly implied.)
Why did the dad see a giant rifle hovering over his house in the dream for no reason?
Why was the cop a recovering addict when that contributed nothing to the story?
Why was the junkie character a thing when he could have been cut?
Why was the cop's girlfriend in the movie only for her to get cheated on and crashout?
Why would the witch leave her stick where Alex could find it, even with the room being protected?
Why didn't the dad turn the light on when he went into the basement?

These are just some issues I can rattle off, I'm sure you can think of others. This was a very creative and rewarding movie that I'm glad I saw, but it is disappointing that the script needed a clear rewrite.

Yes the witch feeds of the life force of others. Seems like she has been doing it well before the Oregon trail era by the conversation she had with the prinicple.


I dont have an answer for the rifle sequence dream. Its very odd.

Its called giving characters nuance and making them feel like characters. The cop being a recovering drug and alcohol addict also plays into the part of chasing the junkie away.

James the junkie is used a means to shows us what's in Alex home earlier. James is known to break in into places and when he breaks into the house we see what's inside the house the whole time before the reveal. It shows little details like the canned soup which we later see why there were so many. You couldn't have done that any other way, thats why he is integral to the story.

The girlfriend is just added to once again show more about Paul's flawd character. He is a piece of shit but we see why.

She had many sticks. Alex happens to find it in the bathroom. She didnt think Alex had it in him to get inside the room. She had made him complacent and was manipulating him the entire time and thought he wouldnt be brave enough to go through with it

author: @graylykan2739
text:
Looking at the poster, and hearing its premise, reminded me a bit of the NoSleep/Creepypasta "The Disappearance of Ashley, Kansas", a short story about a small town called Ashley that suffered from strange and disturbing supernatural occurrences in the days that led up to its disappearance (a small black opening in the sky that got bigger, the road of the only town leading into Ashley not leading into Ashley anymore, a family mysteriously disappearing after attempting to leave Ashley by car, the sun no longer rising, a massive fire on the horizon, and the town's children disappearing). But then the town's children, even the dead ones and every single person who ever went missing prior, came back looking for their loved ones, but all of them came back wrong and started burning every house they entered, and the whole town vanished overnight with only a burning fissure in the earth remaining. 
I also thought "Weapons" was based on a recent book by Stephen King.

text: I hated this movie. Plot holes, pointless characters with no development, no strong character motives, graphic scenes just for the sake of it, inconsistent pacing, this list goes on for me. It played like a comedy the second half. Why is the evil aunt doing any of this? She’s just a parasite? Plus all of the adults seem pretty chill about all these kids missing. I’m going to watch Prisoner’s again to cleanse my brain. Some of the shots were really cool, but I don’t understand the hype this movie is getting. 2/10
